summary: I was born in Grass Valley, California, on April 7, 1934. My father was a gold miner there for 55 years before working in the county road department. Growing up in Grass Valley shaped my early life before I eventually moved on to other places for work and union activities.

I spent about 44 years working on the waterfront and was a member of Local 63 for 35 years within the International Longshore and Warehouse Union. I’m currently involved with the pensioners group in Southern California and serve on its executive board. My brothers and I were all connected to the ILWU in different locals, and it became a big part of my working life starting in 1968.

Before joining the ILWU, I worked for Shell Chemical for ten years. I was very active in the Oil, Chemical and Atomic Workers International Union there, serving as secretary of our local group. I often clashed with the company during that time, which eventually led me to leave and head down to the waterfront for new opportunities.

entity: Oil, Chemical and Atomic Workers International Union
summary: While working at Shell Chemical, I was very active in the Oil, Chemical and Atomic Workers International Union. Our plant was 100 percent unionized, and I served as secretary of the local for several years. It was a tough period with frequent clashes against the company, which eventually made me decide to move on to waterfront work.

I served in the United States Army from 1954 to 1955. After my discharge, I went to Oregon to work with my brother in logging. My time in the Army was a brief but important chapter before I started my civilian working life in Oregon and later in California.

After leaving the Army in 1955, I moved to Oregon and worked in logging near Glendale with my brother Ken. I was setting chokers behind his Caterpillar machinery for about a year until the woods closed down. That closure pushed me to move to Southern California where my other brothers were already involved in union work.

I came down to Southern California after logging in Oregon shut down because my two brothers were already working in the longshore unions there. One brother was in Local 63 and the other in Local 13. Southern California became my home base where I joined the clerks union in 1968 and later became active in the pensioners group.

My brother Chuck worked in Stockton, California, for three years after high school starting in 1949. He often talked about his experiences working on ships there and about Harry Bridges. Stockton was part of the early union stories I heard before I joined the ILWU myself.

I first heard about Harry Bridges from my brother Chuck, who worked on ships and talked about him a lot. This was back when Chuck was in Stockton around 1949. Harry Bridges was a significant figure in the longshore union world, and his name came up often as part of the union culture I eventually joined.
